title: Pressure-cooker chicken stock
subtitle: Forty-five minutes under pressure, four hours of flavour.
excerpt: A roasting carcass or a kilo of wings cooked under pressure with onion, carrot, celery, and peppercorns. The pressure cooker pulls in forty-five minutes what an open pot manages in four hours, and the stock is darker and more deeply flavoured for it.
sourceNotes: Pressure-cooker stock dates to the consumer pressure cookers of the 1930s, refined in the modern multi-cooker generation that arrived in the 2010s. Modernist Cuisine (Myhrvold, 2011) covers the chemistry: the higher pressure raises the boiling point of water to roughly 121°C, accelerating the breakdown of collagen and the extraction of gelatin without driving off aromatic compounds. The result is a stock with the body of a four-hour reduction in a fraction of the time.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well for up to six months in 500 ml portions. Ice-cube trays work for small portions used in pan sauces.
recipe.batchNotes: Make a double batch if your pressure cooker is large enough. The flavour holds for a week in the fridge.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Stock keeps in the fridge for four days. The fat solidifies on top as a seal; scrape it off before reheating.

content.text: Stock is the single most useful thing to keep in the freezer. Risottos, soups, gravies, braises, the splash that saves a dry pan all want it, and shop-bought stock cubes only carry you so far. The pressure cooker collapses the time cost of making it from a half-day to under an hour.

A roast-chicken carcass works perfectly. So does a kilo of raw wings or thighs, which give a paler stock with more body. Both methods follow the same shape: bones, aromatics, water, pressure, strain, cool.

items.prepNote: carcass from a roast bird, or 1 kg raw wings

content.text: A 6-litre pressure cooker is ideal. A stovetop pressure cooker works the same way; the times are identical at high pressure.

Break the chicken carcass into pieces small enough to fit below the pressure cooker's maximum-fill line. Add {{onion}} halved onion, {{carrot}} cut carrots, {{celery}} cut celery sticks, {{garlic}} of smashed garlic, {{bay-leaves}} of bay, {{thyme}} of thyme, and {{black-pepper}} of black peppercorns. Pour in {{water}} of cold water; the bones should be covered but the total volume must not exceed the maximum-fill mark.

Lock the lid. Bring to high pressure and cook for 45 minutes. For a stovetop cooker, lower the heat once the pressure indicator rises so the cooker maintains pressure with the faintest hiss; the cooker should not be venting hard. Turn off the heat and let the pressure release naturally — about 20 minutes. Do not quick-release: a fast release agitates the stock and produces a cloudier result.

Open the lid carefully, lifting it away from you. Ladle the stock through a fine-mesh sieve into a clean bowl or jug. Press lightly on the bones to extract the last of the liquid; do not push hard or the stock turns cloudy. Discard the bones and aromatics. Cool the stock to room temperature within two hours, then refrigerate. The fat rises and sets as a pale yellow cap that lifts off cleanly with a spoon.

items.cause: Released pressure too fast, or pressed too hard when straining.
items.fix: Cloudy stock still tastes correct. To re-clarify, simmer with an egg white whisked in for ten minutes, then strain through a muslin-lined sieve.

items.symptom: Stock is thin and lacks body
items.cause: Bones were too few for the water volume, or cooking time too short.
items.fix: Return the strained stock to the pressure cooker, add another carcass or 500 g of wings, and pressure-cook for a further 30 minutes.

items.cause: Aromatics overwhelmed the chicken; usually too much garlic or onion for the bone weight.
items.fix: Halve the aromatic vegetables next time. The 1:2:1 ratio of bones to water to aromatic vegetables is reliable.

items.cause: Pressure-cooker base ran dry, usually because too little water went in.
items.fix: Always cover the bones with water and respect the maximum-fill line. A burnt base ruins the whole batch; start over.

items.cause: Maximum-fill line exceeded, or the gasket is worn.
items.fix: Pour out some water and try again. Replace the gasket annually if you use the cooker weekly.

content.text: Refrigerate for up to four days; the fat cap seals the stock from oxidation and lifts off when you need to use it. Freeze in 500 ml portions for soups and braises, or in ice-cube trays for small pan-sauce splashes. Frozen stock keeps for six months without quality loss.

Home stock-making fell out of common practice in British kitchens during the post-war stock-cube era; the pressure cooker is what makes it practical to bring back. A weekly batch from one roast carcass produces more stock than most households cook with in a week, and the cost is whatever the chicken cost in the first place. The flavour difference against shop-bought is not subtle.
